8	/-!
9	# Higher direct images `Rⁱ f_*` of quasi-coherent sheaves (`i ≥ 1`)
10	
11	This file treats the higher derived direct images `Rⁱ f_* F` for `i ≥ 1`,
12	complementing the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of `Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean`.
13	
14	Throughout, `f : X ⟶ S` is a morphism of schemes and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of
15	modules on `X`. The `i`-th higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F` is the `i`-th right
16	derived functor of the pushforward functor `f_*` applied to `F`.
17	
18	The four main declarations are:
19	
20	* `AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage` — the higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F`.
21	* `AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent` — `Rⁱ f_* F` is
22	  quasi-coherent when `f` is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
23	* `AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero` — `Rⁱ f_* F = 0` for
24	  `i ≥ 1` when `f` is affine.
25	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_higherDirectImage_isIso` — flat base change
26	  for the higher direct images (`i ≥ 1` case).
27	
28	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex`.
29	
30	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE (quasi-coherence of
31	higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing), 02KH (flat base change).
32	-/
33	
34	universe u
35	
36	open CategoryTheory Limits
37	
38	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
39	
40	open Scheme.Modules
41	
42	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
43	
44	/-- The `i`-th higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F` of a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`
45	along a morphism `f : X ⟶ S`, defined as the `i`-th right derived functor of the
46	pushforward functor `f_*` applied to `F`.
47	
48	For `i = 0` this recovers the ordinary pushforward `R⁰ f_* F = f_* F`.
49	
50	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Definition of `Rⁱ f_*`. -/
51	noncomputable def higherDirectImage [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
52	    (f : X ⟶ S) (i : ℕ) (F : X.Modules) : S.Modules :=
53	  ((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F
54	
55	/-- **Quasi-coherence of higher direct images** (Stacks 02KE, part (1)).
56	If `f : X ⟶ S` is quasi-compact and quasi-separated and `F` is a quasi-coherent
57	`O_X`-module, then `Rⁱ f_* F` is a quasi-coherent `O_S`-module for every `i`.
58	
59	The proof (Stacks 02KE) is local on `S`, reducing to `S` affine; one then runs the
60	induction principle for quasi-compact opens of the quasi-compact quasi-separated
61	`X`: the affine base case uses relative affine vanishing
62	(`higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero`) together with quasi-coherence of `f_*` for
63	the `i = 0` part, and the inductive step uses the relative Mayer–Vietoris
64	sequence, whose terms are quasi-coherent.
65	
66	Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KE ("Quasi-coherence of higher direct images"). -/
67	theorem higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
68	    (f : X ⟶ S) [QuasiCompact f] [QuasiSeparated f] (i : ℕ) (F : X.Modules)
69	    (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) :
70	    (higherDirectImage f i F).IsQuasicoherent := by
71	  -- Proof (Stacks 02KE) via the induction principle for q.c. opens of the q.c.q.s.
72	  -- scheme `X`, using relative affine vanishing in the affine base case and the
73	  -- relative Mayer–Vietoris sequence in the inductive step.  Needs the explicit
74	  -- description of `Rⁱ f_*` as the sheafification of `V ↦ Hⁱ(f⁻¹ V, F|…)` and the
75	  -- Mayer–Vietoris infrastructure, both currently absent from Mathlib.
76	  sorry
77	
78	/-- **Relative affine vanishing** (Stacks 02KG).
79	If `f : X ⟶ S` is an affine morphism and `F` is a quasi-coherent `O_X`-module,
80	then `Rⁱ f_* F = 0` for all `i ≥ 1`.
81	
82	The proof (Stacks 02KG): `Rⁱ f_* F` is the sheafification of
83	`V ↦ Hⁱ(f⁻¹ V, F|_{f⁻¹ V})`; for `V` affine, `f⁻¹ V` is affine (as `f` is affine),
84	so the higher cohomology of the quasi-coherent `F|_{f⁻¹ V}` vanishes. As the affine
85	opens form a basis of `S`, the sheafification of a presheaf vanishing on a basis is
86	zero.
87	
88	Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KG ("Relative affine vanishing"). -/
89	theorem higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
90	    (f : X ⟶ S) [IsAffineHom f] (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) (F : X.Modules)
91	    (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) :
92	    IsZero (higherDirectImage f i F) := by
93	  -- Proof (Stacks 02KG): on affine opens `V ⊆ S` the preimage `f⁻¹ V` is affine,
94	  -- so `Hⁱ(f⁻¹ V, F|…) = 0` for `i ≥ 1` (vanishing of higher cohomology of a
95	  -- quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine scheme), hence the defining presheaf of
96	  -- `Rⁱ f_* F` vanishes on a basis and its sheafification is zero.  Needs the
97	  -- explicit `V ↦ Hⁱ(f⁻¹ V, F|…)` description and affine-cohomology vanishing,
98	  -- both currently absent from Mathlib for `Scheme.Modules`.
99	  sorry
100	
101	/-- **Flat base change for the higher direct images** (Stacks 02KH, `i ≥ 1` case).
102	Given a cartesian square
103	```
104	  X' --g'--> X
105	  |f'        |f
106	  v          v
107	  S' --g---> S
108	```
109	with `F` a quasi-coherent `O_X`-module, `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact and
110	quasi-separated, the canonical base-change map is an isomorphism
111	```
112	  g^*(Rⁱ f_* F) ≅ Rⁱ f'_* ((g')^* F)
113	```
114	for every `i ≥ 1`.
115	
116	The proof (Stacks 02KH) reduces to `S = Spec A`, `S' = Spec B` with `A → B` flat;
117	by quasi-coherence (`higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent`) the two sides correspond
118	to the `A`-module `Hⁱ(X, F)` and the `B`-module `Hⁱ(X_B, F_B)`, and one shows
119	`Hⁱ(X, F) ⊗_A B ≅ Hⁱ(X_B, F_B)` via base change of a Čech complex (separated case)
120	and the Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence (quasi-separated case), using flatness
121	of `A → B`.
122	
123	We state the isomorphism as `Nonempty (… ≅ …)`: the canonical higher base-change
124	map (the `i ≥ 1` analogue of `pushforwardBaseChangeMap`) is not yet constructed.
125	
126	Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH ("Flat base change"), the `i ≥ 1` case. -/
127	theorem flatBaseChange_higherDirectImage_isIso
128	    [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules] [HasInjectiveResolutions X'.Modules]
129	    (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
130	    (h : IsPullback g' f' f g) [Flat g] [QuasiCompact f] [QuasiSeparated f]
131	    (i : ℕ) (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) :
132	    Nonempty ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj (higherDirectImage f i F) ≅
133	      higherDirectImage f' i ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F)) := by
134	  -- Proof (Stacks 02KH): local on `S'`, reduce to `S = Spec A`, `S' = Spec B`,
135	  -- `A → B` flat.  By quasi-coherence the two higher direct images are the
136	  -- tilde-modules of `Hⁱ(X, F)` and `Hⁱ(X_B, F_B)`, and pullback along `g`
137	  -- corresponds to `- ⊗_A B`; the comparison `Hⁱ(X, F) ⊗_A B ≅ Hⁱ(X_B, F_B)`
138	  -- follows from base change of the Čech complex (separated case) and the
139	  -- Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence (q.s. case), using flatness.  Needs the
140	  -- Čech-cohomology / spectral-sequence infrastructure for `Scheme.Modules`,
141	  -- currently absent from Mathlib.
142	  sorry

noncomputable def Gobj (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) : X.Modules :=
  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)

example (F : X.Modules) {Y1 Y2 : Over X} (g : Y2 ⟶ Y1) :
    Gobj F Y1 ⟶ Gobj F Y2 := by

  · exact eqToHom (by rw [w])
  · exact (pushforward Y2.hom).map ((pullbackComp g.left Y1.hom).hom.app F)

((pushforward f).obj M).presheaf.map i.op = M.presheaf.map ((Opens.map f.base).map i).op := rfl

@[simp]
lemma pushforward_map_app (φ : M ⟶ N) (U : Y.Opens) :
    ((pushforward f).map φ).app U = φ.app (f ⁻¹ᵁ U) := rfl

/-- The pullback functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes. -/
def pullback : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules :=
  SheafOfModules.pullback f.toRingCatSheafHom

/-- The pullback functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes
is left adjoint to the pushforward functor. -/
def pullbackPushforwardAdjunction : pullback f ⊣ pushforward f :=
  SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction _

section

attribute [local instance] preservesBinaryBiproducts_of_preservesBinaryCoproducts
  preservesBinaryBiproducts_of_preservesBinaryProducts

instance : (pullback f).IsLeftAdjoint := (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).isLeftAdjoint
instance : (pushforward f).IsRightAdjoint := (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).isRightAdjoint
instance : (pushforward f).Additive := Functor.additive_of_preservesBinaryBiproducts _
instance : (pullback f).Additive := Functor.additive_of_preservesBinaryBiproducts _

end

variable (X) in
/-- The pushforward of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
to the identity functor. -/
def pushforwardId : pushforward (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
  SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _

@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_hom_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_inv_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl

variable (X) in
/-- The pullback of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
to the identity functor. -/
def pullbackId : pullback (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
  SheafOfModules.pullbackId _

variable (X) in
lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom :
    conjugateEquiv .id (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 X)) (pullbackId X).hom =
      (pushforwardId X).inv :=
  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom _

/-- The composition of two pushforward functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
identify to the pushforward for the composition. -/
def pushforwardComp :
    pushforward f ⋙ pushforward g ≅ pushforward (f ≫ g) :=
  SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _

@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_hom_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_inv_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl

/-- The composition of two pullback functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
identify to the pullback for the composition. -/
def pullbackComp :
    pullback g ⋙ pullback f ≅ pullback (f ≫ g) :=
  SheafOfModules.pullbackComp _ _

/-- Pushforwards along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
def pushforwardCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pushforward f ≅ pushforward g :=
    pushforwardNatIso _ (Opens.mapIso _ _ (hf ▸ rfl)) ≪≫
      SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by cat_disch)

@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_hom_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
    ((pushforwardCongr hf).hom.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl

@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_inv_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
    ((pushforwardCongr hf).inv.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl

/-- Inverse images along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
def pullbackCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pullback f ≅ pullback g :=
  eqToIso (hf ▸ rfl)

lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv :
    conjugateEquiv ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).comp (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
      (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ g)) (pullbackComp f g).inv =
    (pushforwardComp f g).hom :=
  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv _ _

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
@[reassoc]
lemma pseudofunctor_associativity :
    (pullbackComp f (g ≫ h)).inv ≫
      Functor.whiskerRight (pullbackComp g h).inv _ ≫ (Functor.associator _ _ _).hom ≫
        Functor.whiskerLeft _ (pullbackComp f g).hom ≫ (pullbackComp (f ≫ g) h).hom =
    eqToHom (by simp) := by
  let e₁ := pullbackComp f (g ≫ h)
  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerRight (pullbackComp g h) (pullback f)
  let e₃ := Functor.isoWhiskerLeft (pullback h) (pullbackComp f g)
  let e₄ := pullbackComp (f ≫ g) h
  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.inv ≫ (Functor.associator _ _ _).hom ≫ e₃.hom ≫ e₄.hom = _
  have : e₃.hom ≫ e₄.hom = (Functor.associator _ _ _).inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₁.hom :=
    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_assoc.{u}
      h.toRingCatSheafHom g.toRingCatSheafHom f.toRingCatSheafHom)
  simp [this]

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
@[reassoc]
lemma pseudofunctor_left_unitality :
    (pullbackComp f (𝟙 Y)).inv ≫
      Functor.whiskerRight (pullbackId Y).hom (pullback f) ≫ (Functor.leftUnitor _).hom =
        eqToHom (by simp) := by
  let e₁ := pullbackComp f (𝟙 _)
  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerRight (pullbackId Y) (pullback f)
  let e₃ := (pullback f).leftUnitor
  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom = _
  have : e₁.hom = e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom :=
    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_id_comp.{u} f.toRingCatSheafHom)
  simp [← this]

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
@[reassoc]
lemma pseudofunctor_right_unitality :
    (pullbackComp (𝟙 X) f).inv ≫
      Functor.whiskerLeft (pullback f) (pullbackId X).hom ≫ (Functor.rightUnitor _).hom =
        eqToHom (by simp) := by
  let e₁ := pullbackComp (𝟙 _) f
  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerLeft (pullback f) (pullbackId _)
  let e₃ := (pullback f).rightUnitor
  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom = _
  have : e₁.hom = e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom :=
    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_comp_id.{u} f.toRingCatSheafHom)
  simp [← this]

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
attribute [local simp] pseudofunctor_associativity pseudofunctor_left_unitality
  pseudofunctor_right_unitality Bicategory.toNatTrans_conjugateEquiv
  conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom Adjunction.ofCat_comp conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv in
/-- The pseudofunctor from `Schemeᵒᵖ` to the bicategory of adjunctions which sends
a scheme `X` to the category `X.Modules` of sheaves of modules over `X`.
(This contains both the covariant and the contravariant functorialities of
these categories.) -/
@[simps! obj_obj map_l map_r map_adj

def pseudofunctor :
    Pseudofunctor (LocallyDiscrete Scheme.{u}ᵒᵖ) (Adj Cat) :=
  LocallyDiscrete.mkPseudofunctor
    (fun X ↦ Adj.mk (Cat.of X.unop.Modules))
    (fun f ↦ .mk (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f.unop).toCat)
    (fun _ ↦ Adj.iso₂Mk (Cat.Hom.isoMk (pullbackId _))

    (fun _ _ ↦ Adj.iso₂Mk (Cat.Hom.isoMk (pullbackComp _ _).symm)

end Functorial

  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)

noncomputable def Gmap (F : X.Modules) {Y1 Y2 : Over X} (g : Y2 ⟶ Y1) :

  simp only [Over.id_left]
  ext U : 1

/-! ### Push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules` — object and morphism bricks

The geometric backbone above is lifted to a cosimplicial `O_X`-module by the
*relative-direct-image functor on the over-category*
```
  G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules,   (Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F,
```
sending an `X`-scheme `p : Y ⟶ X` to the pushforward along `p` of the pullback
`p^* F`. We record here the two *pre-coherence* bricks of `G` — its action on
objects (`pushPullObj`, the planner's `Gobj`) and on morphisms (`pushPullMap`,
the planner's `Gmap`) — both axiom-clean and free of any functor law. The functor
laws `G(𝟙) = 𝟙` and `G(g ≫ h) = G(h) ≫ G(g)` are a *consumer* of the
pushforward/pullback composition coherence (`pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` and
their unitor/pentagon identities) and are deferred; see the file-end note. -/

/-- The object map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`,
`(Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F`. Sends an `X`-scheme `Y` (with structure map `Y.hom : Y.left
⟶ X`) to the pushforward along `Y.hom` of the pullback of `F`. Project-local
object brick of the {\v C}ech push–pull functor (the planner's `Gobj`). -/
noncomputable def pushPullObj (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) : X.Modules :=
  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)

/-- The morphism map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`. For a
morphism `g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁` of `X`-schemes (so `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`, the
over-triangle `Over.w g`), the contravariant functor produces the restriction
comparison `pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂`. It is the three-step composite:
the unit `η` of the adjunction `pullback g.left ⊣ pushforward g.left`, the
pushforward comparison `(pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, and the pushforward
of the pullback comparison `(pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, glued by two
`eqToHom` transports along the over-triangle `Over.w g`. No functor law is used:
this is a reusable pre-coherence brick (the planner's `Gmap`). -/
noncomputable def pushPullMap (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
    pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂ :=
  (pushforward Y₁.hom).map

      (Over.w g))

# AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean

## Summary
- **Declarations added (2):** `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj` (object brick),
  `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap` (morphism brick) of the push–pull functor
  `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`, `(Y,p) ↦ p_* p^* F`. Both **axiom-clean**
  (`#print axioms` = `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`; verified via
  `lean_verify`).
- **Declarations blocked (0 added):** the functor laws `pushPullMap_id` /
  `pushPullMap_comp` — attempted via two routes, deferred (see below). NOT added
  (no sorry left).
- **Sorry count (whole file): 4 → 4** (unchanged: `CechNerve`, `CechAcyclic.affine`,
  `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`, `cech_flatBaseChange` — all pre-existing,
  out of scope this iter). My additions introduced **zero** new sorries.

This is exactly the objective-3 deliverable (the planner's `Gobj`/`Gmap` bricks),
renamed to `pushPullObj`/`pushPullMap` for clean namespace hygiene (correspondence
documented in their docstrings).

## pushPullObj (def, ~line 122)
- **Approach:** Direct definition `(pushforward Y.hom).obj ((pullback Y.hom).obj F)`.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.

## pushPullMap (def, ~line 130)
- **Approach:** Forward term composite of the 3 steps + 2 `eqToHom`s, exactly per
  the planner's recipe:
  1. `(pushforward Y₁.hom).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g.left).unit.app M₁)`
     where `M₁ = (pullback Y₁.hom).obj F`;
  2. `(pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app ((pullback g.left).obj M₁)`;
  3. `(pushforward Y₂.hom).map ((pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app F)`;
  glued by `eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj _) (Over.w g))` and
  `eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward Y₂.hom).obj ((pullback q).obj F)) (Over.w g))`.
- **Key gotcha:** `refine ?_ ≫ ?_ ≫ …` with holes FAILS (intermediate objects
  under-determined → "don't know how to synthesize implicit argument"). Must write
  it as a **fully-applied forward term**, and give each `eqToHom` an **explicit
  `congrArg` proof** so its object equality is determined (not inferred from
  neighbours). `Over.w g : g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.

## API confirmed (Mathlib, `AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean`)
- `Scheme.Modules.pushforward : (X ⟶ Y) → X.Modules ⥤ Y.Modules`
- `Scheme.Modules.pullback : (X ⟶ Y) → Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules`
- `pushforwardComp f g : pushforward f ⋙ pushforward g ≅ pushforward (f ≫ g)`
- `pullbackComp f g : pullback g ⋙ pullback f ≅ pullback (f ≫ g)`
- `pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f : pullback f ⊣ pushforward f`
- Sectionwise: `pushforwardComp_hom_app_app := rfl` (i.e. `pushforwardComp.hom.app`
  is sectionwise `𝟙`), `pushforwardId_hom_app_app := rfl`.

## pushPullMap_id / pushPullMap_comp (NOT added — deferred, documented in a
   `/- … -/` note after `pushPullMap`)
- **Approach 1 (sectionwise `ext`):** `unfold; simp only [Over.id_left]; ext U : 1`
  works but exposes `(pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left)).unit.app … |>.app U`
  — the pullback adjunction unit is **not** sectionwise trivial (pullback =
  sheafify ∘ extendScalars), so the sectionwise goal does not collapse. FAILED to
  close.
- **Approach 2 (abstract coherence `simp`):** plain `simp` and
  `simp [pseudofunctor_right_unitality, conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom,
  Adjunction.unit_naturality, Functor.map_comp]` make **no progress** on the head
  `(pushforward Y.hom).map (unit_{𝟙}.app M) ≫ (pushforwardComp 𝟙 Y.hom).hom.app …`.
  FAILED to close.
- **MATHLIB-COHERENCE FINDING (updates the planner's premise):** the
  pushforward/pullback composition coherences the laws consume **already exist in
  Mathlib** — the whole `pseudofunctor : Pseudofunctor (LocallyDiscrete Schemeᵒᵖ)
  (Adj Cat)` packaging, with named 2-cell coherences
  `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` (`conjugate(pullbackComp.inv) = pushforwardComp.hom`),
  `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom`, and the three unitor/pentagon lemmas
  `pseudofunctor_left_unitality`, `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`,
  `pseudofunctor_associativity`. So `pushPullMap_id`/`pushPullMap_comp` do **not**
  strictly require the project-local Sq1 (`sheafificationCompPullback_comp` in
  `TensorObjSubstrate.lean`) — contrary to the iter-262/263 "needs Sq1" coupling
  belief. They are provable from Mathlib alone.
- **Next step (precise decomposition for a focused fine-grained pass):**
  - `pushPullMap_id`: rewrite the head `unit ≫ (pushforwardComp 𝟙 Y.hom).hom` as
    the conjugate-mate of `(pullbackComp 𝟙 Y.hom).inv` via
    `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv`, then collapse the `𝟙`-leg using
    `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom` + `pseudofunctor_right_unitality` (their `X :=
    Y.left`, `f := Y.hom`), discharging the two `eqToHom` transports against the
    unitality `eqToHom (by simp)` RHS.
  - `pushPullMap_comp`: same mate rewrite, then
    `pseudofunctor_associativity` (their `f := h.left`, `g := g.left`, `h := Y₁.hom`)
    to reassociate the three pullback comparisons, plus
    `Adjunction.unit_naturality` to slide the inner unit past the outer pushforward.
  - The friction is purely the `eqToHom`-through-`Over.w` bookkeeping + matching the
    `respectTransparency false` setting the Mathlib unitor lemmas are stated under
    (`set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false`). Budget ~80–150 LOC.

## Why I stopped
**Real progress:** 2 axiom-clean declarations added — `pushPullObj` (~line 122)
and `pushPullMap` (~line 130). These are precisely the objective-3 (non-preemptive,
mathlib-build) deliverable.

**Partial progress on the next step:** the functor laws `pushPullMap_id` /
`pushPullMap_comp` were attempted via the sectionwise route and the abstract
coherence-`simp` route; both fail to auto-close. The genuine route is the
adjunction-mate calculus assembled from the Mathlib `pseudofunctor` coherence
lemmas (named above) — a bounded but multi-step `eqToHom`-bookkeeping proof that I
did not complete this iter. They are **not added** (no sorry); the file remains at
its 4 pre-existing out-of-scope sorries. The brick is explicitly non-preemptive, so
I delivered the requested object/morphism bricks and the precise law decomposition
rather than open-ending a large coherence proof here.

**Blueprint:** no `\lean{}` target corresponds to `pushPullObj`/`pushPullMap` (the
chapter describes `G` only in prose in `sec:cech_three_part`); no marker action.
The downstream `CechNerve` hole still needs `G` = these two bricks + the two
functor laws above to assemble.

1	# Archon Project

3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8

19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundlePullback
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence
8	
9	/-!
10	# Coherence of locally trivial line bundles (A.2.c engine)
11	
12	This file is the **iter-256 Lane engine** file-skeleton for the cheap, on-path
13	half of the line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme
14	embedding (`chap:Picard_QuotScheme`, `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`).
15	
16	The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a line
17	bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a finitely
18	presented `𝒪`-module. The embedding's input is a point of the relative Picard
19	scheme `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, whose carrier is the **locally trivial** predicate
20	`IsLocallyTrivial` of `chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback` (the project-side stand-in
21	for an invertible sheaf). The chapter proves
22	`IsLocallyTrivial M ⟹ M.IsFinitePresentation`, together with a rank-one /
23	flatness record. The general implication "`M` tensor-invertible `⟹` `M` locally
24	free of rank one" (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma 0B8M when stalks are
25	local) is *not* crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
26	finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
27	
28	## Status (iter-258: 1 sorry → 0 locally; redirected to shared root)
29	
30	The engine file is now **locally `sorry`-free**. The last remaining ingredient,
31	the over↔restrict bridge `chartOverIso`, has been redirected to the general
32	`Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`
33	(the iter-258 shared-root pivot). That general bridge is a complete one-line
34	composite; its remaining mathematical content (the three isos `overEquivalence`,
35	`restrictOverIso`, `unitOverIso`) lives in the shared-root file, where the dual
36	lane (`DualInverse.lean`) consumes the same construction. Once those three close,
37	this engine becomes fully axiom-clean with no further edits here.
38	
39	Of the five pinned declarations, four were closed axiom-clean and the fifth
40	(`chartPresentation`) is complete modulo the (now redirected) `chartOverIso`:
41	
42	* `exists_trivializing_cover` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): repackage the pointwise
43	  `IsLocallyTrivial` existential as an indexed affine cover.
44	* `chart_free_rank_one` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): the chart-local trivialisation.
45	* `chartPresentation` — built as `unitPresentation.ofIsIso chartOverIso.inv`; its
46	  `IsFinite` rides on the `ofIsIso` instance. Reduces to `chartOverIso`.
47	* `isFinitePresentation` — CLOSED modulo `chartOverIso`: assembles the
48	  `QuasicoherentData` from the cover + `chartPresentation`, `shrink`s the index
49	  into the site-object universe, and feeds `IsFinitePresentation.mk`.
50	* `isFiniteType` — CLOSED modulo `chartOverIso`: the Mathlib
51	  `IsFinitePresentation → IsFiniteType` instance.
52	
53	Two reusable axiom-clean bricks were added: `freeUnitIso` (`free PUnit ≅ unit R`)
54	and `unitPresentation` (the canonical finite presentation of the unit module,
55	one generator / no relations), with a `Presentation.IsFinite` instance.
56	
57	**`chartOverIso` (now redirected, no local `sorry`)** — is the over↔restrict
58	trivialisation bridge: an isomorphism `M.over U ≅ unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`
59	transporting the given scheme-level trivialisation
60	`e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ unit (↑U).ringCatSheaf`. The two source categories are
61	equivalent via the open-immersion site equivalence
62	`Opens.overEquivalence U : Over U ≌ Opens ↥U` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Over`);
63	lifting that equivalence to the level of *sheaves of modules* is the shared root
64	`Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence` of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`,
65	and the bridge is the general `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` built there. This file
66	now delegates to that construction (the same wall the dual lane consumes). See
67	`informal/chartOverIso.md`.
68	
69	The 5 pinned declarations are:
70	
71	1. `IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover` (`lem:lbc_trivializing_cover`) —
72	   repackage the pointwise existential of `IsLocallyTrivial` into an indexed
73	   trivialising affine cover.
74	2. `IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation` (`lem:lbc_chart_presentation`) — the
75	   trivial finite free presentation of `M.over U` on a trivialising chart.
76	3. `IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation` (`thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation`) —
77	   the main theorem: assemble a `QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising cover
78	   and feed it to `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk`.
79	4. `IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType` (`cor:lbc_isFiniteType`) — finite type +
80	   quasi-coherence, immediate from finite presentation.
81	5. `IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one` (`lem:lbc_rank_flat`) — the chart-local
82	   rank-one free record.

84	## Site instances (the chief de-risk; see `rem:lbc_site_instances`)
85	
86	Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation` / `QuasicoherentData` are stated
87	under three site hypotheses on the slice topologies `J.over U` of the underlying
88	topology `J = Opens.grothendieckTopology X` of `X.ringCatSheaf`:
89	
90	```
91	[∀ U, (J.over U).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
92	[∀ U, HasWeakSheafify (J.over U) AddCommGrpCat]     -- (resp. HasSheafify)
93	[∀ U, (J.over U).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
94	```
95	
96	The de-risk outcome (whether these resolve for `X.ringCatSheaf`) is recorded in
97	the task result and probed by the `#check` commands at the end of this file.

100	
101	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_LineBundleCoherence.tex`.
102	Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
103	(Tag 01CS, invertible modules), "Modules of finite presentation", and
104	Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M).
